metadata.title: Installs, .env & Credentials
metadata.purpose: Video 2 of 'Brutalist — Claude for Video Production' on @NikBearBrown. The setup video: .env and credentials, what npx/pip/venvs are and why the toolkit uses them, the paid services, and the highest-leverage step — cloning your voice. Meta: built by the toolkit, about the toolkit. Basis doc: docs/Installs.md.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — the master clock; every beat conforms to its measured audio
metadata.reformat: center-cut, focus-aware; pantry/<bid>-916.* is the human replacement slot; -916 overrides honored

beats.narration_text: Before you make a single video with this toolkit, you set up — once. Keys, credentials, a couple of tools. This is that setup. Not every install: just the parts people trip on, and the one that saves you the most time.
Like this. Copy dot env dot example to dot env. It's gitignored, so your keys never get committed. Open it, fill in what you want, leave the rest blank.

props.code: $ cp .env.example .env    # gitignored — your keys never get committed
props.caption: one file, blank = off

beats.narration_text: Two tools need a word of explanation. First, N-P-X. It runs a Node package's command-line tool without installing it globally — and it picks the exact version the project pins. No global copy to go stale, nothing to clean up.

production_viz.label: npx — run, don't install
production_viz.mechanic: Left: a messy pile of stacked 'global install' boxes, one greyed and marked 'wrong version'. Right: a single 'npx' box that runs (checkmark) then fades away, pulling the exact pinned version tag from the project. Contrast a permanent stale pile vs a transient, correct run.

beats.narration_text: That's how every Remotion render happens here. N-P-X remotion render. Same command on every machine, always the version this project was built against.

props.code: $ npx remotion render src/index.ts BarChart out/chart.mp4
props.caption: npx = the version this project pins

beats.narration_text: Second, pip — Python's installer. It pulls the libraries the scripts import: Manim for animation, librosa for timing, Pillow for the slates, and the rest. One command installs the whole pinned set.

production_viz.label: pip — the libraries
production_viz.mechanic: A 'PyPI' cloud at top; arrows down into the project delivering labeled library chips: manim, librosa, faster-whisper, Pillow, vtracer, google-api. One command ('pip install -r requirements.txt') feeds them all in. The point: pip pulls the libraries the scripts import.

beats.narration_text: And a virtual environment — a venv. It's an isolated Python: its own interpreter, its own packages, in a folder inside the project. That's how we can hold numpy under two here without touching numpy anywhere else on your machine.

production_viz.label: venv — an isolated Python
production_viz.mechanic: A bubble/enclosure labeled '.venv' containing its own python + package chips, including a highlighted 'numpy < 2'. Outside the bubble, a system python holding 'numpy 2.x'. The bubble keeps the two from colliding. The point: the numpy<2 pin can hold here without touching the rest of your machine.

beats.narration_text: Make it once, activate it, and ask the doctor. Setup prints a readiness table — green means the libraries are exactly where the scripts expect them.

Four services cost money, and none is required to start. ElevenLabs does voice and narration — the important one. Higgsfield generates A-I image and video. Fal is an optional image path. And YouTube publishing is free — just quota'd.

production_viz.label: the paid services
production_viz.mechanic: Three cards left-to-right: ElevenLabs = 'voice + narration' (HIGHLIGHTED, the important one), higgsfield = 'AI image / video', fal = 'optional image path'. A fourth card set slightly apart: YouTube = 'free — just quota'd'. Exactly one accent, on ElevenLabs.

beats.narration_text: Here's the single biggest time-saver in the whole toolkit. Record about thirty minutes of your own voice, once, and clone it. Narrate live and you pay a tax every session — finding the mic, killing the noise, setting levels, redoing the flubbed line. Thirty minutes gone before you keep one good take. The clone moves that cost to once. After that, every video narrates itself from the beat sheet, in your voice, instantly.

production_viz.label: CLONE YOUR VOICE
production_viz.mechanic: The hero beat — give it the most care. Split-scale graphic. LEFT, labeled '30 min EVERY session': a repeating stack of mic / levels / retake icons multiplying, exhausting. RIGHT, labeled '30 min ONCE': a single record session -> a 'voice clone' chip -> many videos each narrated from it, instantly. Big title 'CLONE YOUR VOICE' in EB Garamond. Teal on the 'once' side as the kept/good accent; the 'every session' side is the wasted-cost side.

beats.narration_text: Yes — the clone mispronounces some words. So do I. It's a review note like any other: catch it, spell that one word out in the beat's text, and move on. A rounding error against never fighting a microphone again.

production_viz.label: it mispronounces some words — so do you
production_viz.mechanic: Light, not alarming — the caveat as a shrug. A word bubble showing a mispronounced word marked in red, then a phonetic respelling fix that resolves it to teal, tagged with a small 'review note' chip. The point: it's a review note like any other — fix that one beat's text and move on.

beats.narration_text: When you're set, two commands tell you exactly where you stand. Setup, for the dependencies. And art keys — it checks every key you've set, live and free, without spending a thing.

That's the short version. The full video also covers .env — one file, youtube/credentials/<channel>/ and Claude Code runs inside the venv — watch Installs, .env & Credentials for the whole story. The link is right below.
